content.text: GenAI services have a fundamentally different latency profile from traditional API services. A conventional API reads from a database and returns in 10–100ms. A GenAI service adds an LLM generation step that takes 500ms to 5 000ms depending on model size, prompt length, and completion length — an order-of-magnitude longer. This is not a performance defect; it is an inherent property of autoregressive token generation.

The second difference is that GenAI latency is composed: authentication, query rewriting, vector retrieval, re-ranking, LLM prefill, token generation, and guardrail evaluation each contribute independently measurable latency. An NFR document that specifies only end-to-end latency cannot diagnose where a SLA breach originates or which component needs optimisation.

A single end-to-end latency target is not an NFR — it is a constraint. The NFR must decompose that constraint into per-component budgets that the team can engineer against and the monitoring system can alert on independently.

attrs.alt: GenAI request latency breakdown: Auth/GW (~20ms), Query Rewrite (~200ms), Retrieval + Re-rank (~300ms), LLM Prefill / TTFT (~700ms), Token Generation (~1500ms), Guardrails (~80ms) — total P50 ~2800ms

content.text: The Four Latency Metrics That Matter

TTFT is the elapsed time from request receipt to the first generated token leaving the LLM. It is the latency the user experiences before any visible response appears in a streaming interface. TTFT is dominated by the LLM prefill phase — the time to process the entire input prompt before generation begins. Prefill time is roughly linear in prompt length.

TTFT is the primary UX signal for interactive GenAI applications. A TTFT above 1 500ms makes users feel the application is hanging. Streaming interfaces exist specifically to make the TTFT feel lower by delivering partial output as soon as the first token is ready.

TBT is the latency between successive tokens during generation. For autoregressive LLMs, TBT is approximately constant for a given model and hardware configuration. Fast models (GPT-4o-mini, Claude Haiku) typically produce 40–80 tokens per second via SaaS APIs; larger models are 10–25 tokens per second. A TBT of 25ms (40 tok/s) produces text at roughly twice reading speed — imperceptible.

Total generation latency is TTFT plus (TBT × number_of_output_tokens). For a 500-token response at 40 tok/s, generation takes 12.5 seconds. The NFR must therefore constrain maximum response length as well as speed — otherwise a correct-but-verbose LLM can technically satisfy the token-rate NFR while still failing the end-to-end target.

E2E latency covers the full path from client request to the last byte of the response arriving at the client. It adds retrieval, guardrail, and network round-trips to the generation latency. E2E is what users experience and what SLAs reference, but it is not actionable for optimisation without the per-stage breakdown.
First token event minus request start

Throughput for a GenAI service is measured in requests per second (RPS) and in tokens per second (TPS). These are related but not interchangeable: 10 RPS of 2 000-token requests is a very different load from 10 RPS of 200-token requests.
Number of complete query-response cycles per second
Primary capacity signal; drives autoscaling decisions
Sum of prompt token counts across all concurrent requests per second
Drives embedding API capacity and LLM prefill load
Sum of generated token counts per second
Drives LLM generation throughput and downstream streaming capacity
Drives memory requirements on GPU inference; vLLM PagedAttention capacity
Requests waiting for an available slot
Leading indicator of throughput saturation before latency degrades visibly

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import NamedTuple

class RequestProfile(NamedTuple):
    avg_input_tokens: int
    avg_output_tokens: int
    peak_rps: float

@dataclass
class ThroughputNFR:
    peak_rps: float
    avg_input_tokens: int
    avg_output_tokens: int
    max_concurrency: int
    max_queue_depth: int

    @property
    def peak_input_tps(self) -> float:
        return self.peak_rps * self.avg_input_tokens

    @property
    def peak_output_tps(self) -> float:
        return self.peak_rps * self.avg_output_tokens

    @property
    def total_peak_tps(self) -> float:
        return self.peak_input_tps + self.peak_output_tps

    def validate_against_provider_limit(self, provider_tpm_limit: int) -> bool:
        peak_tpm = self.total_peak_tps * 60
        return peak_tpm <= provider_tpm_limit * 0.8  # 20% headroom

# FNOL GenAI system NFR
fnol_throughput = ThroughputNFR(
    peak_rps=25,
    avg_input_tokens=2500,   # system prompt + context + query
    avg_output_tokens=600,
    max_concurrency=50,      # in-flight requests at peak
    max_queue_depth=20,      # max waiting before 503
)

print(f"Peak input TPS:  {fnol_throughput.peak_input_tps:,.0f}")
print(f"Peak output TPS: {fnol_throughput.peak_output_tps:,.0f}")
print(f"Peak TPM:        {fnol_throughput.total_peak_tps * 60:,.0f}")

# Check against OpenAI GPT-4o limit (e.g., 800k TPM for tier 5)
within_limit = fnol_throughput.validate_against_provider_limit(800_000)
print(f"Within provider TPM limit: {within_limit}")
LLM Provider Rate Limits as NFR Constraints

SaaS LLM providers impose token-per-minute (TPM) and request-per-minute (RPM) rate limits that are tier-dependent and per-model. These limits are external constraints on throughput NFRs: the maximum achievable throughput is bounded by the provider limit, not by the application's horizontal scaling.
Much higher than LLM; rarely the bottleneck
RPM limit is the binding constraint at low token counts
Up to quotas negotiated with Azure
Per-region, per-deployment quotas; quota increase via support ticket

NFR setting for throughput therefore requires knowing the organisation's current provider tier and the timeline for tier advancement. If the target throughput exceeds current tier limits, the NFR document must include a dependency on tier upgrade as a pre-production gate.
A throughput NFR that exceeds the LLM provider's rate limit is not an engineering problem — it is a procurement and commercial problem. Surface it in the blueprint, not in a post-go-live incident.
With the E2E latency target and the per-stage breakdown defined, the engineering team can allocate a latency budget to each stage and design each component to fit within its budget:
Run BM25 and vector search concurrently rather than sequentially. Reduces retrieval latency from sum to max.
Approximate nearest-neighbour parameters (ef_search in HNSW, nprobe in IVF) trade recall for latency. Tune to meet the retrieval latency budget.
Queries that repeat within a session or across sessions (FAQ-type queries) can be cached at the retrieval layer with a semantic cache (vector similarity match to cached queries).
Every 1 000 prompt tokens adds roughly 50–150ms to TTFT on a 70B model. Trim system prompts; use hierarchical retrieval to avoid over-retrieving context.
Anthropic and OpenAI both cache the prefix of long prompts between calls (minimum 1 024 tokens for Anthropic). Reusing cached prefixes reduces prefill cost and latency by 50–80%.
Use a smaller model (Claude Haiku, GPT-4o-mini) for low-stakes, latency-sensitive tasks; use a larger model only where quality gain justifies the latency increase.
The single largest lever for total generation latency is maximum output token count. Setting max_tokens is both a quality and a latency control:

If the computed max_output_tokens is unrealistically small, the E2E latency target is incompatible with the expected response quality. This incompatibility must surface in the NFR discussion, not after deployment when users complain about truncated answers.

Standard load testing tools (k6, Locust, JMeter) require adaptation for GenAI services because throughput is measured in tokens, not requests, and because streaming responses require connection-holding behaviour that many tools handle poorly:

import asyncio
import statistics
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime

@dataclass
class GenAILoadTestResult:
    scenario_name: str
    target_rps: float
    duration_seconds: int
    results: list[dict] = field(default_factory=list)

    @property
    def actual_rps(self) -> float:
        return len(self.results) / self.duration_seconds

    @property
    def ttft_p95_ms(self) -> float:
        ttfts = [r["ttft_ms"] for r in self.results if r.get("ttft_ms")]
        return sorted(ttfts)[int(len(ttfts) * 0.95)] if ttfts else 0

    @property
    def e2e_p95_ms(self) -> float:
        e2es = [r["e2e_ms"] for r in self.results if r.get("e2e_ms")]
        return sorted(e2es)[int(len(e2es) * 0.95)] if e2es else 0

    @property
    def error_rate(self) -> float:
        errors = sum(1 for r in self.results if r.get("error"))
        return errors / len(self.results) if self.results else 0

    def meets_nfr(self, nfr: LatencyBudget, max_error_rate: float = 0.01) -> bool:
        return (
            self.ttft_p95_ms <= nfr.ttft_p95_ms
            and self.e2e_p95_ms <= nfr.e2e_p95_ms
            and self.error_rate <= max_error_rate
        )

async def run_load_test(
    endpoint: str,
    rps: float,
    duration_s: int,
    query_sampler: callable,
) -> GenAILoadTestResult:
    result = GenAILoadTestResult(
        scenario_name=f"rps={rps}",
        target_rps=rps,
        duration_seconds=duration_s,
    )
    interval = 1 / rps
    start = asyncio.get_event_loop().time()

    async def single_request():
        query = query_sampler()
        t0 = asyncio.get_event_loop().time()
        ttft_ms = None
        try:
            import httpx
            async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=30) as client:
                async with client.stream("POST", endpoint, json={"query": query}) as resp:
                    async for chunk in resp.aiter_text():
                        if ttft_ms is None and chunk.strip():
                            ttft_ms = (asyncio.get_event_loop().time() - t0) * 1000
            e2e_ms = (asyncio.get_event_loop().time() - t0) * 1000
            result.results.append({"ttft_ms": ttft_ms, "e2e_ms": e2e_ms})
        except Exception as exc:
            result.results.append({"error": str(exc)})

    tasks = []
    while asyncio.get_event_loop().time() - start < duration_s:
        tasks.append(asyncio.create_task(single_request()))
        await asyncio.sleep(interval)
    await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
    return result

Load test scenarios for GenAI must include a ramp-up phase (to detect queue saturation) and a sustained phase (to detect thermal throttling on GPU or provider rate limit exhaustion). The load test should use a realistic query distribution sampled from the expected production workload, not a single repeated query (which would hit the semantic cache and produce optimistically low latency).
The following template captures the complete latency and throughput NFR set for a GenAI service in a form suitable for inclusion in the solution blueprint:

# nfrs/latency-throughput.yaml
latency:
  e2e:
    p50_ms: 3000
    p95_ms: 4500
    p99_ms: 7000
    measurement: client-side including network RTT
  ttft:
    p50_ms: 800
    p95_ms: 1500
    alert_threshold_ms: 2500
    measurement: server-side span (request receipt to first SSE token)
  per_stage_budgets:
    auth_gateway_ms: 50
    query_rewrite_ms: 400
    retrieval_ms: 400
    reranking_ms: 200
    llm_prefill_ms: 1500    # = TTFT minus preceding stages
    guardrails_ms: 200
  streaming: true           # TTFT delivered to client; rest streamed

throughput:
  peak_rps: 25
  sustained_rps: 15         # 15-min sustained baseline
  avg_input_tokens: 2500
  avg_output_tokens: 600
  max_output_tokens: 800    # hard cap enforced by orchestration layer
  max_concurrency: 50
  max_queue_depth: 20       # beyond this, 503 Service Unavailable

provider_constraints:
  llm:
    provider: openai
    model: gpt-4o
    required_tier: tier_5   # 800k TPM minimum
    current_tpm_limit: 800000
    safety_headroom_pct: 20
  embedding:
    provider: openai
    model: text-embedding-3-large
    required_tpm: 2500000   # peak_rps * avg_input_tokens * 60

load_test:
  scenarios:
    - name: ramp
      start_rps: 5
      end_rps: 30
      duration_s: 300
    - name: sustained_peak
      rps: 25
      duration_s: 900
    - name: burst
      rps: 50
      duration_s: 60
  pass_criteria:
    ttft_p95_ms: 1500
    e2e_p95_ms: 4500
    error_rate_pct: 1

Latency and throughput NFRs for GenAI services must be more detailed than for traditional APIs because the latency is composed across multiple stages and is dominated by a non-deterministic LLM generation step. Effective NFR authoring requires decomposing the E2E target into per-stage budgets, constraining output token length to make the budget achievable, and validating that the required throughput fits within LLM provider rate limits before committing to a SLA.
is the primary UX signal for interactive applications — target below 1 500ms at P95.
Decompose E2E latency into per-stage budgets; only then can the team engineer and monitor against the NFR.
Output token length is a latency lever — set max_tokens based on the generation budget, not model defaults.
Validate throughput NFRs against LLM provider rate limits before design is complete, not at load-test time.
Load tests must use realistic query distributions and include ramp-up, sustained peak, and burst scenarios.
